learningObjective: Discover that curved areas can be approximated with rectangles, and understand why we need infinitely many rectangles for exact area - the approximation improves as rectangles multiply but never reaches perfection at any finite count
ahaMoment: The approximation error shrinks but never quite vanishes at any finite n; there's always visible gap between rectangle tops and curve, requiring infinite rectangles for exactness
targetMisconception: Students don't see why we can't just use a few rectangles to get the exact area

miniSpec.title: Slider Increases Rectangle Count Under Curve
miniSpec.narrative_wrapper: Students see a curved line above the x-axis with a few tall rectangles underneath trying to fill the space. They drag a slider labeled 'number of rectangles' and watch the rectangles multiply and shrink in width, fitting the curve more snugly. The total shaded area value updates as they drag.
miniSpec.manipulated_variable: Slider controlling n, the number of rectangles in the partition
miniSpec.primary_visual_feedback: Rectangles redraw immediately with new widths and heights, appearing directly beneath the curve in the same coordinate system where the slider sits below the graph
miniSpec.goal_structure: Free exploration with suggested milestones: try n=4, n=10, n=50, n=100 to observe convergence toward a target value displayed separately
miniSpec.causal_chain_exposed: Width of each rectangle (Δx = (b-a)/n), height of sample rectangle, individual rectangle area, running sum of all rectangle areas, and gap between approximation and true area
miniSpec.trace_strategy: None—previous rectangle configurations disappear to avoid clutter, since the learning focuses on the current state's accuracy rather than the path of improvement
miniSpec.aha_moment: The approximation error shrinks but never quite vanishes at any finite n; there's always visible gap between rectangle tops and curve, requiring infinite rectangles for exactness

interactionContract.student_changes: drags the number of rectangles (n) from 1 to 100+
interactionContract.student_sees: rectangles fill the region; the sum updates and converges toward the true area as n increases

modelVariables.riemannSums_n: number of rectangles in the partition, student-manipulated via slider or drag dial (range 1-200)
modelVariables.riemannSums_a: left bound of integration (carried over from previous section, locked/frozen)
modelVariables.riemannSums_b: right bound of integration (carried over from previous section, locked/frozen)
modelVariables.riemannSums_riemannSum: derived: total area of all rectangles (left Riemann sum)
modelVariables.riemannSums_trueArea: derived: the true area under the curve using high-precision integration
modelVariables.riemannSums_error: derived: absolute difference between riemannSum and trueArea
modelVariables.riemannSums_errorPercent: derived: error as a percentage of trueArea
modelVariables.riemannSums_explored: boolean: whether the student has interacted with the n control

assertions.description: On load, the visualization shows a small number of rectangles with visible gaps between rectangle tops and curve - posing the problem of imprecise approximation
assertions.visualCheck: small number of wide rectangles visible under curve, obvious gaps between rectangle tops and the curve, error gauge shows non-trivial error

assertions.description: The number of rectangles (n) can be changed via the quality dial or slider
assertions.visualCheck: rectangles multiply from 4 to 20, each becoming narrower

assertions.description: As n increases, the Riemann sum converges toward the true area
assertions.visualCheck: rectangles now fit curve snugly, gaps barely visible, error gauge shows small error

assertions.description: The bounds a and b are carried over from the previous section and cannot be changed by the student
assertions.visualCheck: bounds remain fixed at a=0 and b=8, only rectangles change

assertions.description: Even at high n values, the error never quite reaches zero - the aha moment
assertions.visualCheck: error gauge shows tiny but non-zero error, demonstrating that perfection requires infinite rectangles

invariants.description: The width of each rectangle is always (b-a)/n
The number of rectangles is always at least 1
The left bound is always less than the right bound
